sections.text: The comic ballad *The Place You Come from Is Where I Was Born* concerns a brief, adulterous affair between a man and a woman who grew up together but did not marry. The seduction begins (lines 1-14) with images that evoke the idea of closeness and familiarity.
Then, in several lines (15-23) he poetically affirms the complementarity between sexes.

The metaphorical argument continues (lines 24-50), as the man constructs figurative images of lovers and lovemaking (lines 24-28, 36-50) while the woman answers with her own playful metaphors (lines 29-35).

The seduction concludes with the man's praise of the woman's beauty and her womanhood (lines 51-59), her figurative acquiescence (line 60), and the singer's literal affirmation of her words (line 61).

The lovemaking occurs outside the village in a grove of trees near the fields where women do agricultural work (lines 62-63). It proceeds from literal description of the act (64-65) to emblematic praise for female anatomy (lines 66-69) to metaphorical description of physical sensation (lines 70-72).

Pillow talk follows. The man begins with praise (lines 73-74), then recalls his image of masculine labor (of lines 21-23) to assert the strength of his feeling (lines 75-79), and finishes with more praise of her beauty and an assertion of his passion, this time by comparing it to a spiritual experience (lines 80-84).

The woman responds with figurative praise for the male anatomy (lines 85-86) and follows with parallel declarations of passion drawn from a female realm of work (lines 86-97). The pillow talk ends (line 98) with the woman's playful enunciation of the familiar greeting that the man proposed as part of his seduction (see line 27).
The lovemaking ends with a literal description of the couple's actions (99-101) paralleling that at the beginning of the action (see lines 63-65).

The woman then offers a bit of food to the man, cutting and peeling a piece from one of the yams she had been harvesting — the kind of snack shared among intimates or given to a child, but here presented honorifically on a banana-leaf platter (lines 102-105). The man begins to refuse it, for it could be seen as food unworthy of his social status, but then he reminds himself of the ethos expressed in a particular proverb and agrees to eat (lines 106-108).

The two lovers part and walk a short distance from each other, but then turn and call (lines 109-112), a reflection of a previously proposed seductive metaphor (see lines 40-49). At first, the couple's public exchange language is a veiled reference to their intimacy (lines 113-115). But then the man uses a combination of literal speech and insulting imagery to boast his conquest of, and declare his contempt for, his erstwhile lover (lines 116-120).

The woman responds bravely, attributing his disgraceful ethics to low birth and deploring the social shame he has brought her (lines 121-23).

Then she challenges the aptness of the man's insulting calabash image by contrasting it, on the one hand, with honorific cattle-based imagery for the same referent (lines 124-27), and on the other hand, to the calabash image's literal meaning (lines 128-33). The man curtly reiterates the aptness of his calabash image (line 134).
The woman carefully stores her yams at home (lines 135-41), then travels and meets some men and makes her accusation (lines 142-47).

She tells the men the story of the encounter and her lover's subsequent insult, and she recounts her arguments against his rhetorical description (lines 148-61). The men, however, agree with the insulting lover (162).
She takes her accusation to court, but the judges again agree with the man (lines 163-69). She takes her case to a higher court, but the result is the same (lines 170-76).
The woman begins to cry, when suddenly the king appears and asks her for the source of her woe (lines 177-81).

She tells her story in more detail, explaining to the king about her childhood together with the lover, their plans for marriage, and the disappointment of these plans when the woman's father arranged a marriage with someone else (lines 182-189).

She explains she had just emerged from her bridal seclusion (line 190-3) and then recounts the events and revisits the rhetorical arguments she has articulated before (lines 194-203).
She describes her ascent through the courts, the negative results at each level, and her final anger and despair (lines 204-19).

The king ponders the case only briefly, then decides it in favor of the woman (lines 220-21) and explains the basis of his judgment by explicating the metaphors in the previously used praise epithets (lines 222-235.2).

The king determines fines for the judges who ruled erroneously in the lower courts (lines 236-246). He devises two punishments for the false lover, one corporal (lines 247-250) and one culinary (lines 251-58), and declares that the basis of the man's crime is sexual ignorance (lines 253 and 258; but the king apparently errs here — see line 70).
The king summons the woman's husband, who declares his respect with proper praise (lines 259-63), is deemed knowledgeable on this basis, and receives a culinary reward (lines 265-73).

The king concludes his judgment with an observation (lines 274-81) that, were it not for the misfortune of its placement, the vulva's call would be louder and more compelling than that of the king's own drum.
